questions.text: I set off toward an unfamiliar destination and navigate as I go.
I experience nostalgia as a deep, aching longing rather than a gentle wistfulness.
I feel that speed of execution justifies centralized decision-making.
I handle commitments as they surface throughout the week without a formal review.
I believe that decisions about people's welfare should be informed by evidence and analysis, not by the emotional atmosphere in which they are made.
I believe that the persistence of my moral concern — the fact that it does not fade quickly — is a quality I want to preserve.
I find that my emotional responses don't tend to snowball or escalate.
I feel that dressing well and presenting myself at my best is an investment in my future.
I believe that personal mastery is one of the most rewarding pursuits in life.
I believe that the practice of emotional steadiness during distressing events is a discipline whose value becomes more apparent with every situation that tests it.
I would downsize my own living situation to free up resources for people who need help.
I think that composure during emotionally difficult situations is an essential quality for anyone who wants to make good decisions under pressure.
I thrive when I have the authority to make things happen.
I find that my emotional responses are rarely strong enough to interfere with my concentration.
I believe that a strong focus on personal achievement leads to a well-lived life.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is what ensures that my values remain connected to the concrete realities of other people's lives.
I think of my future self as someone I am actively building conditions for.
I detect when someone uses vague language like 'some people think' to express their own opinion without owning it.
I find that I automatically register who is nervous in a social setting.
I resist templates because each situation deserves a unique response.
I research purchases carefully, comparing options and reading reviews before buying.
I believe that a good day is one where I gave more than I received.
I would turn down a promotion if it meant my colleagues would be worse off.
I let the group find its own course, even if I see a different path.
I build in checkpoints during complex work to verify I'm still on the right track.
I notice that my skin flushes or goes pale in direct response to what I'm feeling.
I feel that my job is to point the way, even if others resist.
I do not usually notice when a person glances at their companion for confirmation before committing to a plan.
I experience a new environment as a barrage of stimulation that takes time to process.
I help the team set milestones together and support everyone in reaching them.
I believe the world would be easier to navigate if people just said what they meant.
I see my presence in a group as primarily about providing support.
I hold the view that my system processes emotions efficiently — they arrive, inform me, and dissipate.
I do not naturally notice when someone is managing their image in a social situation.
I find that the moral pull of another's pain is something I experience as a direct call to action, not as an abstract consideration.
I find that I naturally notice when someone is being strategically vague.
